coredata.dc:title: Nanohybrid optical electronic nose based on zinc oxide/red carbon dots for ammonia sensing at room temperature using surface plasmon resonance on a smartphone platform

coredata.dc:description: This study reports the development of an optical electronic nose sensor designed for the highly sensitive detection of ammonia vapor at room temperature, utilizing zinc oxide/red-emitting carbon dot (ZnO/R-CD) nanohybrid films. The detection mechanism is based on the monitoring of refractive index changes in the ZnO/R-CD films upon exposure to ammonia vapor, employing the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) technique integrated with a smartphone platform. The results showed that a ZnO film thickness of 200 nm provided the highest sensitivity. In addition, under blue light illumination, the sensing response was nine times greater than that of unilluminated film. The proposed sensor also demonstrated the ability to distinguish between five volatile organic compounds (VOCs), including ammonia, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone, by using five different illumination colors on the smartphone display. Among these, ammonia produced the highest sensing response, approximately six times greater than those of the other VOCs. A linear response was observed over an ammonia concentration of 4–33 ppm, with a sensitivity of 1.406 %/ppm. The limit of detection, calculated from the standard deviation of the noise signal, was 0.079 ppm. Furthermore, the sensor exhibited rapid response and recovery times (6 s/54 s) and maintained excellent stability over a period of 45 days. These findings underscore the sensor’s potential for practical applications involving the detection of ammonia vapor, and particularly for monitoring spoiled milk.

head.ce:title: Nanohybrid optical electronic nose based on zinc oxide/red carbon dots for ammonia sensing at room temperature using surface plasmon resonance on a smartphone platform

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the development of an optical electronic nose sensor designed for the highly sensitive detection of ammonia vapor at room temperature, utilizing zinc oxide/red-emitting carbon dot (ZnO/R-CD) nanohybrid films. The detection mechanism is based on the monitoring of refractive index changes in the ZnO/R-CD films upon exposure to ammonia vapor, employing the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) technique integrated with a smartphone platform. The results showed that a ZnO film thickness of 200 nm provided the highest sensitivity. In addition, under blue light illumination, the sensing response was nine times greater than that of unilluminated film. The proposed sensor also demonstrated the ability to distinguish between five volatile organic compounds (VOCs), including ammonia, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone, by using five different illumination colors on the smartphone display. Among these, ammonia produced the highest sensing response, approximately six times greater than those of the other VOCs. A linear response was observed over an ammonia concentration of 4–33 ppm, with a sensitivity of 1.406 %/ppm. The limit of detection, calculated from the standard deviation of the noise signal, was 0.079 ppm. Furthermore, the sensor exhibited rapid response and recovery times (6 s/54 s) and maintained excellent stability over a period of 45 days. These findings underscore the sensor’s potential for practical applications involving the detection of ammonia vapor, and particularly for monitoring spoiled milk.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, there has been growing demand for sensors that are capable of detecting toxic and flammable gases, owing to their extensive range of applications across various sectors, including industrial production, safety, environmental protection, medical diagnostics, and waste management [1–3]. Ammonia, a colorless gas with a pungent odor, is particularly hazardous. Its primary sources include combustion processes in vehicles and chemical plants, and it is also extensively utilized in the manufacturing of fertilizers, and as an industrial coolant [4,5]. According to the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), exposure to ammonia concentrations exceeding 50 ppm in the workplace can cause significant damage to the human respiratory system [6]. This underscores the need for the development of a gas sensor that is capable of rapidly detecting ammonia in real time.

The detection of ammonia gas using metal oxide sensors has garnered significant attention, due to their ease of synthesis, low cost, robustness, and long-term chemical stability, which render them highly promising for the detection of both chemical and biological molecules [7–9]. Zinc oxide (ZnO) is a commonly employed metal oxide for ammonia detection due to its wide bandgap, high binding energy, excellent thermal stability, and high electron mobility [10–12]. Furthermore, ZnO sensors are advantageous because they can be easily synthesized at low cost, exhibit excellent chemical and mechanical stability, are non-toxic and inexpensive, and demonstrate high photosensitivity. However, the use of ZnO for ammonia gas detection requires operating temperatures within the range 200–400 °C to facilitate the surface separation and adsorption of ammonia molecules. At elevated temperatures, these sensors consume substantial amounts of energy, and may give rise to safety concerns during prolonged operation. To mitigate the energy consumption associated with heating, there is increasing interest in operating these sensors at room temperature, although metal oxide sensors typically exhibit low sensitivity and slow response and recovery times when operated at room temperature. Hence, enhancing the performance of this type of sensor is essential. One promising approach involves incorporating ZnO nanohybrids with carbon-based materials, such as carbon nanotubes and graphene, to create sensors that are capable of real-time detection of ammonia gas at room temperature [13–15]. This approach aims to achieve higher sensitivity, faster response/recovery times, compact size, reliability, and low power consumption.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of spherical, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with a particle size typically less than 10 nm. These materials exhibit a range of advantageous properties, including low toxicity, stable luminescence, easy functionalization, biocompatibility, simple synthesis, and tunable optical characteristics [16–18]. In addition, the large surface area to volume ratio of CDs offers increased numbers of adsorption and desorption sites, which enhances the recovery and response times of these sensors, making them highly effective for vapor detection [19–21]. Despite these promising features, however, there are limited reports on the use of CDs for ammonia sensing [21,22]. Consequently, this study explores the use of CDs combined with ZnO as nanohybrid sensors for optical electronic nose applications, in which the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) technique is employed for the detection of ammonia vapor.

Optical vapor sensing has attracted considerable attention in recent years. Compared to traditional electronic noses, optical sensors, which rely on distinct detection mechanisms, present novel opportunities for vapor detection [23–25]. These sensors offer several advantages, including enhanced sensitivity, broader ranges of measurement, elimination of the need for reference electrodes, increased stability over the sensor’s operational lifespan, and the ability to monitor multiple parameters simultaneously with relatively high efficiency. SPR, a highly sensitive optical technique, is capable of detecting minute changes in the dielectric properties of substances at the sensor surface, and has therefore emerged as a prominent tool for research in ammonia vapor sensing [26,27]. SPR is particularly well-suited to applications involving portable ammonia vapor detection using smartphones, due to the compatibility of the light sources and cameras available in commercial devices. The typical brightness of smartphone screens, ranging from 300 to 500 nits, is sufficient to provide illumination for SPR-based measurements [28,29]. Furthermore, these screens provide the wide-angle illumination necessary for conducting angle-resolved SPR measurements, making them particularly appropriate for ammonia vapor detection applications.

In this paper, we present a hybrid nanosensor composed of a ZnO nanoparticle film, which is fabricated using the sparking method [30], and red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs), which are synthesized via the hydrothermal method from mangosteen leaves as natural materials [31]. The proposed sensor employs SPR as the detection technique, which is coupled with the ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid sensor. SPR is a highly sensitive technique for detecting changes in the dielectric constant at the surface of a ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid sensor upon the adsorption of ammonia vapor. A custom-designed prism is developed to induce SPR in a gaseous medium. This prism is compact enough to be compatible with a smartphone, thus serving as a practical and efficient tool. The excitation light is provided by the smartphone's screen, while the front camera functions as the detector, capturing the light reflected from the sensor film. This design eliminates the need for complex detectors and lighting systems, and reduces the size of the detection setup. Consequently, the device becomes a portable, light-based electronic nose that is suitable for onsite ammonia vapor measurements, offering both ease of use and high efficiency. As a demonstration of its application, the device is employed to measure the spoilage of packaged milk.

Gold-coated glass slides were acquired from Angstrom Engineering (Canada), and zinc wires with a purity of 99.7 % were sourced from Advent Research Materials (UK). Ammonia (25 %), ethanol (99 %), methanol (96 %), isopropanol (95 %), and acetone (95 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The mangosteen used in the study was purchased from a local market in Krabi, Thailand. Deionized water (17.0 MΩ·cm) was utilized as the solvent in all experiments, and all chemicals employed in the study were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The preparation of zinc oxide (ZnO) thin films was carried out using the spark deposition method on a glass substrate coated with a layer of gold 50 nm in thickness (Angstrom Engineering). In the deposition process, eight zinc filaments, each measuring 2 cm in length and 0.38 mm in diameter, and with a purity of 99.97 % (Advent Research Material), were employed, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Spark deposition occurred when a voltage of 7 kV was applied. The spark head traversed an area of 18 × 14 mm2, and deposition was performed for 4 to 14 cycles, resulting in film thicknesses ranging from approximately 80 nm to 280 nm. Following deposition, the films were subjected to heat treatment at 600 °C for a duration of 1 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and fabrication of ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication procedure for the ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid is depicted in Fig. 1. CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with mangosteen leaves as the carbon source. Initially, 50 g of mangosteen leaves were washed with deionized water and dried at room temperature. The leaves were then cut into small pieces, mixed with 350 mL of deionized water, and blended in a mixer. The resulting solution was filtered through 11 µm filter paper to remove any residual material, and a 40 mL portion of the filtered solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. Following the reaction, the solution was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The dark brown solution was subsequently filtered through 0.45 µm and 0.22 µm microfiltration membranes to remove any remaining large particles. Finally, a 50 µL aliquot of the yellowish-brown CD solution was drop-cast onto the Au/ZnO substrate using a micropipette. The substrate was then left to dry in a desiccator at room temperature for 12 h. The resulting Au/ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid sensing film was then ready for the detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs), including ammonia, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone vapors.

The R-CDs, ZnO, and ZnO/R-CDs films were fabricated for subsequent material characterization. The R-CDs film was prepared by drop-casting 50 µL of the carbon dot (CDs) solution (as described in Section 2.2) onto a glass substrate with dimensions of 25 × 14 mm2. The film was then dried at room temperature in a desiccator for 12 h. For the ZnO film, high-purity (99.7 %) zinc wire was employed for spark deposition directly onto a 25 × 14 mm2 glass substrate. The spark ablation process was conducted for 4 to 14 cycles, followed by annealing at 600 °C for 1 h to improve crystallinity. To prepare the ZnO/R-CDs nanohybrid film, 10 cycles of spark deposition were first performed using zinc wire on a 25 × 14 mm2 glass substrate, followed by annealing at 600 °C for 1 h. After the sample cooled to room temperature, 50 µL of the R-CDs solution was drop-cast onto the ZnO-coated substrate and dried in a desiccator for 12 h.

The morphology of the ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid film was investigated using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (SIGMA HV–Carl Zeiss), while the morphology of the R-CDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100, Japan). For TEM analysis, the samples were prepared by drop-casting the R-CDs onto a TEM grid. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to record the infrared spectra over the range 400–4000 cm−1 for the ZnO film, R-CDs, and ZnO/R-CDs nanohybrid film using an Equinox 55 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The resolution of the measurements recorded was 1.5 cm−1, so that 2520 data points were generated. The chemical composition of the ZnO and R-CD films was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Physical Electronics, PHI 5000 Versaprobe Scanning ESCA Microprobe). The optical properties of the ZnO film, R-CD film, and ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid film were evaluated using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Genesys, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The thickness of the ZnO films were measured with a surface profilometer (Dektak, Bruker).

Fig. 2 (a) presents a 2D ray tracing diagram of the optical coupler designed for use in the SPR device. The coupler consists of several components through which a light beam emitted by the smartphone screen is coupled to a flat convex cylindrical element (Element 1). The light is then expanded into a fan-shaped beam by a convex lens (Element 2), which is directed at the sensing surface at an incident angle of between 45° and 55°. This configuration excites plasmon wave resonance in air, providing optimal sensitivity to refractive index changes in the ambient air medium. The SPR signal reflected from the sensing surface is focused by two convex lenses (Elements 3 and 4) and is subsequently directed to the front camera through the 60° tilted edge of the coupler (Element 5).

The fabrication process of the optical coupler followed the method described in [29], and can be summarized as follows. First, the optical coupler was designed in three dimensions using Fusion 360 (3D CAD/CAM design software, Autodesk, USA), based on the optical configuration shown in Fig. 2(a). Next, the mold master was fabricated using a 3D printer (Flashforge Creator X) with an acrylonitrile–butadiene–styrene (ABS) filament. A silicone mold was then created, and the optical coupler was produced by casting with optical-grade epoxy EPO-TEK® 301–1 (n = 1.5). The completed coupler, with the flow cell positioned on top and optical contact achieved using an immersion oil, is shown in Fig. 2(c).

The flow cell (dimensions: width = 5 mm, length = 12 mm, height = 100 µm) was fabricated using a 3D printer with Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol-modified (PETG) filament, which is chemically resistant. It was then sealed with a 1 mm thick plexiglass sheet, and through-holes were incorporated to enable connection via stainless steel tubes for the inlet and outlet of dry air and vapor. Finally, the flow cell was mounted onto the Au/ZnO/R-CD sensing film using epoxy resin adhesive (Pattex Power Epoxy #23).

Fig. 2(b) illustrates the experimental setup with the optical coupler mounted on a smartphone screen. A Lenovo K910 smartphone, equipped with an attachment and prism cover to block ambient light during measurements, is shown in Fig. 2(c). A linear polarizer plate (Edmund Optics) was positioned on top of the smartphone screen to generate a p-polarized light source.

The light source from the smartphone screen was generated using five distinct colors, each of which was displayed as a floating image on the screen to serve as the illuminating light source. These colors were as follows: Color 1 (R = 255, G = 0, B = 0), Color 2 (R = 255, G = 50, B = 0), Color 3 (R = 255, G = 100, B = 0), Color 4 (R = 255, G = 150, B = 0), and Color 5 (R = 255, G = 200, B = 0).

The bubbler technique for vapor generation, with controlled flow rate provided by an air pump, was employed in this study (Fig. 2(b)). Drying air (relative humidity, Rh = 40 %) flowed through two separate paths. One path directed the air to blow bubbles through the analyte, which included ammonia, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone. The saturated vapors of these substances were then introduced into a flow cell connected to the Au/ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid sensor. The second path delivered dry air for dilution. The vapor concentration of the analyte, expressed in parts per million (ppm), was calculated according to the method described in Ref. [32] as follows:

The vapor sensor testing protocol consisted of the following steps. Initially, the sensor was exposed to dry air, allowing it to stabilize and establish a baseline signal for the measurements. Subsequently, the sensor was exposed to VOC vapor, including ammonia, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone, for a contact time of 2 min. Next, dry air was passed through the sensor for 2 min to facilitate degassing from the sensor surface, thereby allowing the sensor to return to its baseline state in preparation for the next measurement.

This procedure was designed to analyze both the dynamics of the sensor's initial response to VOC exposure and its recovery dynamics in real time. The light intensity values were measured by analyzing the images reflected from the sensor surface using the SPR device integrated with a smartphone. The reflected light, which varied in intensity based on changes in the refractive index at the sensor surface, was captured by the smartphone's camera. The intensity data were then processed and analyzed to evaluate the sensor's response to the exposure of VOCs.

In the development of the optical electronic nose, we leveraged the color changes of a floating image on a smartphone screen, which displayed five distinct colors, referred to here as Colors 1–5. This approach was designed to achieve varying sensitivities to VOCs by exploiting the distinct optical responses generated by each color. The goal was to enable the sensor to detect and analyze vapors with diverse chemical structures, such as ammonia, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone. To enhance the sensitivity and diversity of the data, principal component analysis (PCA), a widely used statistical technique for reducing high-dimensional data to orthogonal principal components, was employed. The incorporation of five different light colors from the smartphone screen increased the variability in the data, thereby improving the sensor's ability to differentiate between various VOCs and ammonia vapor concentrations, which change as the state of spoilage progresses.

The light intensity for each vapor was measured using SPR techniques, and was continuously monitored and recorded. The sensor's response to VOCs exposure was quantified as the change in light intensity relative to the initial intensity. This approach enabled real-time detection and analysis of VOC vapors, providing a solid foundation for differentiating between various vapors and their concentrations.

In an experiment designed to measure spoilage across five different brands of boxed milk (labeled Milk 1 to Milk 5), the milk samples were exposed to ammonia gas, which is released during the spoilage process. The ammonia gas interacted with the sensor, triggering a measurable response that reflected the level of spoilage in each milk sample. This interaction allowed for the real-time monitoring of ammonia vapor concentrations, which served as an indicator of the stage of spoilage for each brand of milk. The sensor’s response to the ammonia exposure was recorded, providing valuable data on the spoilage dynamics and allowing for differentiation between the spoilage rates of the various milk brands.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of sensing film
ce:section.ce:para: The structure of the ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid sensing film, which was coated on the surface of a gold thin film substrate, is shown in Fig. 3 (a). The morphology of the R-CDs was investigated using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and the results revealed that the R-CDs had a nearly spherical shape and were uniformly distributed. The average particle size was approximately 8.13 ± 1.41 nm, with a d-spacing of 0.27 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(b) and 3(c). The size distribution of the particles, which was obtained through a statistical analysis of approximately 100 particles using Image J software, is depicted in the histogram in Fig. 3(d).

A surface analysis revealed that the ZnO films exhibited considerable roughness, which was primarily attributed to the formation of zinc (Zn) nanoparticles after 10 sparking cycles (Fig. 3(e)). Zinc nanoparticles formation occurred through a nucleation mechanism initiated by the application of a high voltage across zinc tips. The resulting spark discharge induced bombardment of the molten zinc surface at both tips by high-energy electrons and ions accelerated in the electric field. This process generated localized high temperatures and pressures, causing the zinc electrodes to melt and form nanodroplets via nucleation. These nanodroplets were subsequently deposited onto a substrate, where they oxidized upon exposure to atmospheric air, forming the ZnO nanoparticle thin film [34]. The films consisted of highly porous, loosely aggregated ZnO nanoparticles. It could also be inferred that the density of the spongy ZnO nanoparticles increased with the number of sparking cycles. Fig. 3(f) presents the cross-sectional morphology of the films obtained after 10 sparking cycles. The cross-section reveals that the ZnO films had an uneven thickness, with an average thickness of approximately 200 nm that was verified by a surface profilometer (Dektak, Bruker). The thickness of the ZnO film increases linearly with the number of sparking cycles, and the corresponding deposition rate is 20 nm per cycle.

Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping diagrams and the surface morphology of the SEM films are shown in Fig. 3(g)–(j), respectively. The EDS mapping diagrams in Fig. 3(h)–(j) display the elemental distributions of zinc, carbon, and oxygen, respectively, while the corresponding EDS spectra are provided in Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information. In terms of film composition, the atomic percentages of zinc and oxygen were found to be 6.87 % and 32.84 %, respectively. Consequently, the Zn/O ratio was 0.21, which is significantly lower than the theoretical value of 1.0. This discrepancy is likely to be due to excess oxygen adsorbed onto the gold. To confirm the elemental composition of the individual layers more precisely, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted, as described in detail later.

The surface composition and chemical states of the ZnO and R-CDs were analyzed using XPS. Fig. 4 (a) and (b) show the XPS spectra of the Zn core and oxygen levels for the ZnO layers prepared over 10 spark cycles, followed by calcination at 600 °C. In Fig. 4(a), the Zn(2p) core level exhibits a symmetric peak, with the Zn(2p3/2) state appearing at 1021.57 eV and the Zn(2p1/2) state at 1044.61 eV. The spin–orbit splitting between the two states is 23.04 eV, a value which is consistent with the binding of Zn2+ to oxygen in the ZnO matrix. This finding is in agreement with the literature, as referenced in [35]. Fig. 4(b) presents the O1s core level, which is deconvoluted into four Gaussian peaks centered at 530.3, 531.1, 531.8, and 533.2 eV. Based on previous XPS studies, the O1s peak is known to exhibit three main binding energy components: the peak at 530.3 eV corresponds to O2− ions in the crystal lattice of ZnO (OLat), and is characteristic of the wurtzite structure, while the peak at 531.8 eV is attributed to O2− ions in the oxygen-deficient region (OVac), and the peak at 533.2 eV corresponds to oxygen adsorbed onto the surface (OAds) [35]. The high binding energy at 533.2 eV may result from the chemisorption or delocalization of oxygen or hydroxyl (OH) groups on the surface of the ZnO thin films [36]. Furthermore, the stoichiometric ratio of the ZxOγ film, with a y/x value of 0.71 (calculated using the area under the peaks and sensitivity factors for Zn and O), is close to the ideal value of 0.68 for ZnO [35]. This indicates the complete formation of the ZnO material in the film. These XPS measurements not only confirm the purity of the prepared nanoparticles but also provide valuable information about the chemical states of both O1s and Zn(2p).

The surface composition of the synthesized R-CDs was analyzed by XPS, as shown in Fig. 4(c) and (d). The XPS spectrum displayed two primary peaks at 285.04 and 532.0 eV, corresponding to C1s and O1s, respectively. The carbon content was determined to be 77.64 %, while the oxygen content was 17.70 %. The atomic ratio between oxygen and carbon was 0.23. Further analysis of the high-resolution C1s spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 4(c)) revealed characteristic peaks at 285.04, 286.6, 287.68, and 289.04 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, CO, and C–OH bonding configurations, respectively [18,37]. Similarly, the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 4(d)) showed peaks at 531.36, 532.40, 533.29, and 534.36 eV, which are attributed to COC, CO, HOH, and COOH groups, respectively [18]. These XPS results provide detailed insights into the surface functionalization and chemical composition of the synthesized carbon dots.

Fig. 5 shows the FTIR spectra of the R-CDs, ZnO, and ZnO/R-CDs thin films, providing valuable insights into functional groups present in these materials. A broad band observed between 3200 and 3500 cm−1 is attributed to the OH stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups. This feature appears in the spectra of both R-CDs and ZnO/R-CDs but is absent in pure ZnO, suggesting the incorporation of hydroxyl functionalities on the surface of the ZnO/R-CDs film. A distinct peak at 2927 cm−1, corresponding to CH stretching vibrations, is present in all three samples. In the R-CDs spectrum, sharp peaks at 1614 cm−1 and 1452 cm−1 are observed, corresponding to CC stretching vibrations and hydroxyl groups in C–OH bonds, respectively [31]. These peaks are also detected in the ZnO/R-CDs film, though slightly shifted to higher wavenumbers (1602 cm−1 and 1427 cm−1), indicating interactions between ZnO and R-CDs within the nanohybrid structure. A peak at 1033 cm−1, corresponding to the COC stretching vibration, is observed exclusively in the R-CDs film. Furthermore, in the region of 400–1000 cm−1, characteristic peaks at 413, 762, and 894 cm−1 are detected in both ZnO and ZnO/R-CDs spectra. These peaks are attributed to Zn–O vibrations and confirm the structural identity of the ZnO phase in the nanohybrid films [38]. Overall, the FTIR results indicate that the films are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, which are crucial for their chemical reactivity and sensing capabilities.

Fig. 6 (a) presents the transmittance spectra of ZnO/R-CD films as a function of light wavelength in the range 300–800 nm. Notably, an absorption peak at 666 nm is observed for the R-CDs, which can be attributed to the hydrogen bond transition, as confirmed by the FTIR results. This absorption peak is likely to be associated with the excited-state energy storage of the CH and C–OH surface states, as supported by previous studies [31]. The emission of R-CDs demonstrates an optimal maximum emission at 671 nm for an excitation wavelength of 410 nm as shown in Fig. S2. It was interesting to observe an additional absorption peak in the ZnO/R-CD film, although this shifted to a redder wavelength at 673 nm. This redshift may be due to the interaction between the ZnO matrix and the R-CDs, which alters the electronic structure and transitions in the material. The deposition of R-CDs on the ZnO film extends the light absorption range of the ZnO film, enhancing absorption in the 300–400 nm range, which can be attributed to the π → π* electron transition of sp2 aromatic carbon domains (CC, carbon core). Absorption in the 400–500 nm range is likely to be due to the n → π* electron transition in unsaturated oxygen-containing groups (CO, CO) on the surface of the R-CDs. To determine the band gap of the optical energy [39], the Tauc method is used. The relationship between the incident photon energy (
h ν
) and the absorption coefficient (
α
) is given by the equation:

is an energy band gap. Fig. 6(b) shows a graph of (

, which is used to directly determine the optical bandgap of the material. From the experimental data, the optical band gaps of the pure ZnO film and R-CDs were found to be 3.93 and 1.81 eV, respectively. The larger band gap of ZnO is consistent with its wide bandgap, characteristic of the material's semiconductor properties. In contrast, the smaller band gap for the R-CDs suggests that the CDs have a distinct electronic structure, potentially due to surface functional groups and defects, which influence their optical properties.

The reduction in the band gap for the ZnO/R-CD nanohybrid film (2.55 eV) can be attributed to the interaction between the ZnO matrix and the R-CDs, which is likely to introduce new energy levels within the bandgap. These intermediate states could facilitate electron transitions at lower energies, thereby reducing the overall bandgap of the composite material. This shift in the optical bandgap is advantageous for applications that require enhanced light absorption, such as in sensing or photocatalysis, where a broader range of wavelengths can be utilized.

Additionally, the post-dialysis samples exhibited enhanced emission under excitation at 415 nm. Specifically, the intensity of I1D increased, while that of I2D decreased significantly (Fig. S2(e)), leading to an approximately threefold increase in the I1D /I2D ratio compared to pre-dialysis conditions (Fig. S2(b)). These results suggest that dialysis effectively removed larger molecular byproducts, without altering the surface states, particularly those associated with lower-energy (longer-wavelength) emissions, such as I2 . Overall, the combined purification steps confirmed that the primary fluorescence of R-CDs originates from intrinsic electronic transitions, predominantly within the red emission range of 674–723 nm.

However, the purification process also resulted in a decrease in the concentration of R-CDs, leading to a significant reduction in overall absorption intensity, particularly at 452 nm (Fig. S1(c)), which corresponds to the blue light absorption region. This reduction in absorption limited the excitation of electrons within the R-CDs, thereby decreasing the generation of charge carriers and, consequently, reducing surface activity. To overcome this limitation, ZnO/R-CD nanohybrids were fabricated using R-CDs purified by polymer filtration alone, ensuring sufficient sensor responsiveness and supporting their potential for vapor sensing applications under blue light illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness optimization and visible light illumination
ce:section.ce:para: The average film thickness of the ZnO/R-CD sensors ranged from 80 to 280 nm when the number of sparking cycles was varied from 4 to 14. This variation in thickness demonstrates the impact of the number of sparking cycles on film growth and morphology. To assess the performance, six ZnO film sensors, prepared with between 4 and 14 sparking cycles, were tested on a 50 nm thick gold thin film coated on a glass slide. This performance evaluation provided insights into how the film morphology, thickness, and porosity influence sensor characteristics such as sensitivity and response time.

Fifty microliters (50 μL) of R-CDs were uniformly dropped onto each ZnO film, and ethanol vapor was then introduced through a bubbling system, as illustrated in Fig. 2(b). Initially, dry air was allowed to flow into the flow cell for 2 min to stabilize the system. Following this, ethanol vapor at a concentration of 15 ppm was introduced for 2 min to simulate exposure to the analyte. To observe the vapor adsorption and desorption processes on the sensor, dry air was again released into the flow cell for 2 min after exposure to ethanol vapor, to allow for desorption of the ethanol from the sensor surface. The sensor's response was monitored by measuring the light intensity reflected from the surface of the film using the SPR device, as depicted in Fig. 2(b). The smartphone was set to capture images every 3 s during the experiment, and these images were subsequently analyzed using the MATLAB program for intensity pattern analysis. All experiments were conducted at room temperature (∼27 °C) under a humidity of approximately 40 %. The sensor's response to ethanol vapor was determined by analyzing the SPR signal, with a specific focus on the intensity variations captured from the reflected light. The response values were quantified by applying the intensity interrogation pattern.

Fig. 7 (a) presents the intensity response curve of the sensor as the number of sparking cycles increases from 4 to 14 cycles. The intensity response of the ZnO/R-CD film steadily increases, reaching a maximum at 10 sparking cycles. However, when the number of sparking cycles is further increased to 12 cycles, the sensing response undergoes a significant decline. The response appears to reach a saturation when the number of sparking cycles is increased to 14, as shown in Fig. 7(b).

This behavior can be attributed to the structural changes in the film during the sparking process. When the number of sparking cycles does not exceed 10, the film retains a well-developed porous structure characterized by three-dimensional interconnected fine particles. This porous structure enhances the surface area and the interaction between the sensor material and the analyte vapor, which improves the sensor's sensitivity. However, as the number of spark cycles exceeds 10, the ZnO nanoparticles may aggregate more heavily, leading to a less porous, denser film structure. This aggregation could reduce the surface area available for adsorption, thereby diminishing the sensor's response. The decreased response observed at 12 cycles is likely to correspond to the point where further sparking does not significantly enhance the film's performance but rather contributes to film densification or other structural limitations. Due to this increased density, the grain boundaries within the agglomerated regions become less accessible to vapor molecules, and the chemically inactive grain boundaries contribute to a reduced interaction with the vapor. Specifically, the depletion zone caused by surface adsorption of oxygen extends less far into the grain structure in the denser films, which results in a decreased amount of vapor being adsorbed onto the surface. Consequently, the sensing response of the film decreases [40].

The ZnO/R-CD film sensor with a ZnO film layer prepared using 10 spark cycles exhibits the highest response to ethanol vapor, as this combination strikes a balance between a porous structure and sufficient surface area for adsorption. Hence, to achieve the best sensor performance in this experiment, a ZnO film sensor prepared with 10 spark cycles was used throughout the subsequent tests.

We also investigated the effect of light excitation on sensor performance under green, blue, and no light illumination, as shown in Fig. 7(c). When the ZnO/R-CD film was excited by blue light from an LED, it exhibited a sensitivity that was four times higher than that of the film excited by green light. Furthermore, the sensitivity was nine times greater than that of the unexcited ZnO/R-CD film, and 1700 times higher than that of the unexcited ZnO film, as shown in Fig. 7(d). We hypothesize that this enhanced sensitivity under blue light excitation is due to the unique interaction between the ZnO/R-CD film and the energy from the blue light. An excitation wavelength of 465 nm (blue light), corresponding to a photon energy of 2.67 eV, which is greater than the band gap of ZnO/R-CDs (2.55 eV), was used to excite electrons from the valence band to the conduction band. The blue light is likely to promote electron excitation within the material, particularly within the R-CDs, leading to an increased charge carrier density and enhanced surface reactivity. This promotes a more efficient adsorption of gas molecules, such as ethanol vapor, thus improving the overall sensor response. In addition, the interaction between the ZnO semiconductor and the R-CDs may facilitate charge transfer processes, thereby further enhancing the sensitivity under specific light conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: VOC sensing of ZnO/R-CDs under blue light illumination
ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone were selected as model VOC analytes to investigate the sensing properties of the proposed optical electronic nose fabricated from ZnO/R-CD films. The ZnO films were subjected to 10 spark cycles, as this value was found to yield the highest sensitivity for ethanol vapor detection. In this experiment, nine different concentrations of analyte vapors were generated using a bubbler evaporation system; more specifically, analyte solutions with volumes ranging from 10 to 2000 µL were mixed with 75 mL of deionized (DI) water, resulting in vapor concentrations ranging from 2 to 800 ppm, as summarized in Table S1. The response of the ZnO/R-CD sensor was measured for the five VOCs to evaluate the capability of the optical electronic nose in terms of distinguishing between the vapors of these compounds. To achieve this, the wavelength of light used for SPR excitation was varied, as demonstrated in the referenced study [41]. In the present work, we utilize the principle that the dielectric constant or refractive index of a ZnO/R-CDs film changes upon vapor adsorption of various VOCs. To distinguish between different VOCs, SPR excitation is induced using five distinct light wavelengths, implemented through five smartphone screen color variations (denoted as Color 1 to Color 5). The resulting changes in SPR spectral intensity over time are recorded as sensorgrams.

The results are presented in Fig. 8 (a)–(e), which illustrate the vapor adsorption measurements for the tested VOCs: ammonia, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone, respectively. The light intensity measurements obtained from the SPR device revealed that the responses of most substances showed an increase in light intensity with rising concentrations of VOC vapor. However, ammonia exhibited anomalous behavior, as the light intensity initially increased for vapor concentrations from 4 to 33 ppm, but subsequently decreased when the vapor concentration was raised to 820 ppm. Furthermore, it was observed that the pattern of changes in the color wavelength on the display, corresponding to variations in signal intensity, differed for each VOC. This suggests the potential for distinguishing between VOCs, thereby supporting the discriminating capability of our optical electronic nose. To further assess this capability, principal component analysis (PCA) was employed in this study.

PCA was utilized for data interpretation as an unsupervised exploratory data analysis technique. In this approach, a set of variables is transformed from a higher-dimensional space into a lower-dimensional one, thereby reducing the dimensionality of the data. This means that the variance of the data can be represented as a weighted sum of the original variables, which are referred to as principal components (PCs). To analyze the sensor response patterns, including VOCs adsorption and desorption dynamics across different light wavelengths and VOCs species, PCA was applied to the temporal sensorgram data. This multivariate approach enables the identification of characteristic response patterns for each VOCs, facilitating effective VOCs discrimination through PCA-based pattern recognition.

The similarity of data corresponding to the same analyte resulted in clustering of the data points in the PCA plot. Fig. 8(f) presents the score plot for PC1 versus PC2, which account for 89.6 % and 3.7 % of the variance, respectively, with a cumulative variance of 93.3 % of the total variance. The PCA graph clearly separates the five simulated VOCs, demonstrating that our optical electronic nose is capable of distinguishing between these VOCs.

From an evaluation of the PCA response, it was observed that the test vapors exhibited varying magnitudes and response directions depending on the screen colors used to excite the SPR waves. The responses of the sensor films to methanol, isopropanol, and acetone vapors were found to be similar, and the data points for these compounds were clearly separated. In contrast, the responses to ethanol and ammonia were significantly more sensitive than those for the other three compounds; notably, ethanol exhibited a nonlinear response at the tested concentrations and was co-localized with methanol, isopropanol, and acetone in the PCA plot.

The response to ammonia had the highest sensitivity, with a linear correlation to concentration in two distinct phases: an initial increase in response as the ammonia concentration ranged from 4 to 33 ppm, followed by a change in direction when the concentration exceeded 80 ppm. It is hypothesized that the order of the responses of the detection films to the various vapors, as observed in the PCA score plot, corresponds to the refractive indices of the sensing film when the vapors are adsorbed.

Mutual interference can occur during the detection of specific vapors in environmental conditions, particularly when mixtures of two or more VOCs are present. PCA score plot shown in Fig. 8(f) reveals that ammonia exhibits a broad distribution across varying concentration levels, following a linear trend. Notably, ammonia is clearly separated from ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone, indicating that interference effects can be identified, and that the concentration of ammonia can be distinguished from other VOCs. However, to quantitatively resolve the concentrations of individual components within such mixtures, the use of partial least squares (PLS) regression is recommended [41].

In contrast, while ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone form distinct clusters in the PCA plot, the inter-cluster distances are relatively small, indicating a high degree of similarity in the sensor responses to these analytes. This similarity may be attributed to the ZnO/R-CDs film responding similarly to these VOCs. To improve the differentiation among these compounds and enhance the detection of potential mutual interference, increasing the number of SPR excitation wavelengths beyond the current five wavelengths may be necessary.

Selectivity studies were also conducted to evaluate the response of the ZnO/R-CD sensor to ammonia vapor at a concentration of 30 ppm, and to ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone vapors at concentrations of 100 ppm. The sensor response was calculated using the equation (IVOCs − Iwater )/Iwater × 100 %), where IVOCs represents the sensor signal in the presence of the respective analyte vapor, and Iwater is the reference signal corresponding to water vapor. The experimental results indicated that the response to ammonia was the highest, as it was approximately six times greater than the responses of the other analytes, despite their concentrations being approximately three times higher than that of ammonia, as shown in Fig. 9 (a). This finding suggests that the proposed sensor exhibits excellent selectivity toward ammonia at room temperature, in comparison to several potentially interfering analytes such as ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone.

The observed enhanced selectivity may be attributed to the differing adsorption capacities of these vapors on the ZnO/R-CDs surfaces. Specifically, the smaller kinetic diameter and lower ionization energy of ammonia facilitate its diffusion within the ZnO/R-CDs, thereby increasing the availability of adsorption sites. This, in turn, leads to a more significant alteration in electrical conductivity or sensor response when compared to other analytes [42].

Fig. 9(b) illustrates the sensing response of the ZnO/R-CD sensor when exposed to ammonia concentrations ranging from 4 to 823 ppm. The changes in the sensing response exhibit a monotonic trend with two distinct phases. Initially, the sensor shows a linear increase in response, ranging from 18 % to 61 % as the ammonia concentration increases from 4 to 33 ppm. However, at concentrations ranging from 44 ppm to 823 ppm, the sensor response begins to decline, decreasing from 57 % to 13 %. A linear relationship (R2 = 0.95) between sensor response and ammonia concentration is observed in the range of 4 to 33 ppm, with the calibration equation given by y = 1.406x + 17.56, as shown in the inset to Fig. 9(b). This equation serves as the standard calibration curve for measuring ammonia vapor concentrations within the range 4–33 ppm. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from the standard deviation of the noise signal as 0.079 ppm. The sensing mechanism, which involves the injection of charge carriers into the sensing material due to its reaction with ammonia, will be discussed in the following section.

Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the changes in intensity, as measured by the SPR device, following exposure of the ZnO/R-CD sensor to 25 ppm of ammonia vapor over six cycles to assess the stability of the sensor. The results indicate consistent sensing properties, with an average intensity value of 63.1 ± 0.51 %. The excellent recovery characteristics illustrate the sensor's good repeatability, with the average response and recovery times calculated from the six cycles. The data presented in Fig. 10(b) demonstrate that the sensor exhibited rapid response and recovery times of approximately 6 s and 54 s, respectively, when exposed to 25 ppm of ammonia vapor. Fig. S3 presents the response and recovery times for ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone vapor. These results highlight the sensor's very fast response and recovery times, which are superior to those of other ammonia sensors, as summarized in Table 1 .

Fig. 10(c) presents the response and recovery curves for the sensor when exposed to ammonia vapor at a concentration of 25 ppm, at relative humidity (Rh) levels of 42 %, 55 %, 62 %, 72 %, and 80 %. As the humidity increased, the measured light intensity values increased by 8 %, 10 %, 21 %, and 31 %, respectively, compared to the values obtained at 42 % RH. These results indicate that humidity has a significant impact on sensor performance, leading to a reduction in sensor efficiency. Specifically, the sensor response decreased from 53.4 % to 11.3 % as humidity increased because the reference light intensity (Iwater ) at higher Rh levels was found to be elevated. This effect is attributed to the adsorption of water and oxygen molecules from the atmosphere, which alters the local refractive index. Hence, to preserve optimal detection efficiency, it is crucial to store the sensors in a manner that prevents exposure to humid air during long-term storage.

Long-term stability is a critical characteristic for vapor sensors. Fig. 10(d) shows the change in the sensor’s response to ammonia vapor at a concentration of 25 ppm after being stored in a cabinet with 40 % humidity for 45 days. The sensor was tested every five days to evaluate its long-term performance. The data demonstrate the stability of the sensor, with the SPR-measured light intensity remaining relatively constant, showing only a 3 % variation from the initial measurement. The sensor response decreased from 53.4 % to 48.9 % over the 45-day period. At an ammonia concentration of 25 ppm, the sensor maintained stable performance, a finding that confirms its practical suitability for long-term use.

To investigate the interaction between ammonia vapor and the ZnO/R-CD film, which may explain the observed changes in the SPR signals, we considered a sensing system designed to detect ammonia vapor concentrations. This investigation can provide insight into the optical properties of the multilayer films in potential application scenarios. In this step, the SPR signal spectrum was simulated using the multilayer Fresnel equation [45,46], and the setup included a prism, a thin gold film, a ZnO/R-CD film, and dry air or ammonia vapor, as described in Eqs. In the simulation, the spectra will account for changes in both the real and imaginary components of the refractive index of the ZnO/R-CD film upon the adsorption of water vapor and ammonia vapor at varying concentrations. These simulated spectra will then be compared with the experimental spectra to gain a deeper understanding of the interaction mechanisms between the ZnO/R-CD film and the vapor analytes, as well as the SPR measurement process.

ZnO is an n-type semiconductor, while CDs are p-type semiconductors. When ZnO was combined with CDs to form a ZnO/CD nanohybrid, the material retained the characteristics of an n-type semiconductor due to the small amount (6 mg/mL) of CDs deposited on the surface of the sensing film [19]. This hybrid structure contains oxygen vacancies, which facilitate the adsorption of atmospheric oxygen. The adsorbed oxygen plays a more significant role in the sensing of ammonia vapor than the oxygen present within the lattice, as evidenced by the XPS results. In air, oxygen molecules are adsorbed onto the surface of the ZnO/R-CDs and capture electrons, forming oxygen ions. This reaction can be described by Eqs. (9) and (10). Following electron capture, the depletion layer in the ZnO/R-CD film widens, leading to an initial decrease in the film’s conductivity [43].

When blue light irradiates the surface of the ZnO/R-CD film, photons are absorbed by the sensor film, leading to the generation of electron-hole pairs, as described in Eq. (11). The production of photoelectrons enhances the chemisorption of oxygen molecules and improves the mobility of charge carriers across the grain boundaries [47]. Simultaneously, the light-induced electrons react with oxygen molecules in the air, resulting in the formation of light-induced oxygen ions. This process is represented by Eqs. (12)–(14).

As the photogenerated electrons enter the conduction band, the electron concentration increases, which in turn reduces the width of the electron depletion layer in the heterojunction. Fig. 11 (a) (spectrum 1) shows the simulated initial SPR spectrum when the ZnO/R-CD film is exposed to dry air. In this simulation, the dielectric constant of the film is represented by n1 = 1.993 + 0.143i, where the real part of the refractive index is calculated using the Ravindra equation (n = 4.16 − 0.85Eg ) [48], which correlates the refractive index to the energy band gap of the film in air (Eg = 2.55 eV). The imaginary component, representing energy loss, is then included to ensure that the simulated spectrum aligns with the experimental results, which exhibit a characteristic broadening. The corresponding light intensity, obtained via intensity interrogation at an incident angle of 49°, is marked as position 1 in Fig. 11(b). Secondly, when water vapor, which served as the reference in the experiment, is introduced into the system, the ZnO/R-CD film absorbs the water vapor, causing a decrease in film’s conductivity [49]. This occurs because water interacts with both the photogenerated holes and electrons in the depletion layer, leading to their delocalization and altering the surface polarization. The conductivity response to water vapor is more pronounced than that observed in dry air, as the reduced conductivity is associated with a decrease in the refractive index of the film. Accordingly, in the simulation, the real part of the refractive index of the film is adjusted to n2 = 1.912 + 0.143i. This change causes the SPR peak to shift toward a smaller incident angle, as shown in spectrum 2 in Fig. 11(a). Consequently, this shift corresponds to a decrease in light intensity, as illustrated at position 2 in Fig. 11(b).

When a small concentration of ammonia molecules is adsorbed onto the ZnO/R-CD film, oxidation of ammonia occurs due to the interaction with the adsorbed oxygen. This reaction results in the removal of hydrogen and the formation of NO, as described in Eq. (14). The NO generated in the gas phase, along with the release of electrons back to the film surface, results in an increase in the film’s conductivity. This behavior contrasts with the second phase, during which the film surface primarily absorbs water molecules. Consequently, the refractive index of the film increases to n3 = 1.934 + 0.143i. The light intensity continues to increase in correlation with increasing ammonia concentration, reaching a maximum at 33 ppm. At this concentration, the refractive index further increases to n4 = 1.969 + 0.143i, causing the SPR peak to shift toward a higher incident angle, as observed in spectra 3 and 4 of Fig. 11(a). This SPR shift leads to a continued increases in light intensity, as shown at position 3 and 4 in Fig. 11(b), respectively.

After the ammonia concentration exceeds 44 ppm, a decrease in light intensity is observed with further increases up to 823 ppm. This behavior is attributed to a reduction in the number of available active sites for vapor molecule adsorption as the concentration rises significantly. Once these active sites become saturated, an additional molecular layer forms on the ZnO/R-CD film surface, leading to a decrease in the film’s conductivity. As a result, the refractive index of the film decreases again, causing the SPR spectrum to shift toward a smaller incident angle, as shown in spectrum 5 of Fig. 11(a). Correspondingly, the light intensity decreases, as illustrated at position 5 in Fig. 11(b).

In addition, we investigated the effect of ambient temperature on the sensor's performance by measuring its response to ammonia vapor at a concentration of 25 ppm in a temperature-controlled chamber. The experiment was initiated at 30 °C, and the temperature was subsequently increased in 5 °C increments. At each step, the sensor response was recorded, and this procedure was repeated over nine cycles, reaching a final temperature of 70 °C. The experimental results, shown in Fig. 12 (a), illustrate the changes in light intensity with increasing temperature. It is observed that the sensing film exhibits an enhanced response to ammonia, with the light intensity increasing from 67 at 30 °C to 149 at 45 °C. However, beyond 50 °C, the response to ammonia becomes nearly indistinguishable from that of dry air, indicating a loss in sensitivity.

To analyze this behavior, SPR spectra were simulated using the multilayer Fresnel equation (Eqs. (4)–(8)). As the temperature increased, the refractive index of the sensing film also increased [50], causing the SPR angle to shift toward higher incident angles, as illustrated in Fig. 12(b). Consequently, when intensity interrogation was performed at a fixed angle of 49°, the intensity response to ammonia appeared to increase. Notably, at temperatures of 45 °C and above, the SPR resonance angle exceeded the angular range of the optical coupler used in this study (45–55°). As a result, the detected light intensity began to saturate at approximately 180 from 55 °C to 70 °C. However, the sensing response decreased from 55 % at 30 °C to 45 % at 45 °C, as shown in the inset of Fig. 12(a). This reduction in sensing response with increasing temperature is consistent with previous studies [51,52] and is attributed to an increase in the reference intensity (Iwater ) caused by the elevated ambient temperature.

Nevertheless, this sensor was designed for optimal operation at room temperature, where its response remains stable. If the sensor is to be deployed in environments with the temperature exceeding 40 °C, temperature-compensation techniques are required to ensure accurate ammonia concentration measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to the detection of milk spoilage
ce:section.ce:para: We evaluated the feasibility of using the proposed ZnO/R-CD sensor in a practical application involving the detection of milk spoilage by sensing ammonia vapor during the spoilage process. In addition to the release of ammonia vapor, carbon dioxide (CO2) is also produced as a result of microbial growth [53]. To evaluate the sensor’s selectivity toward ammonia in the presence of CO2 as an interfering gas, we investigated its response to CO2 by introducing the CO2 gas from a cylinder at a concentration of approximately 120 ppm into a flow cell, alternating with dry air over five cycles. The experimental results, shown in Fig. S5, indicate that the sensor exhibits a very weak response to CO2, with a change in light intensity of only 0.2 (see magnified inset Fig. S4). This response is approximately 300 times lower than that observed for ammonia. These findings confirm that the sensor is highly selective to ammonia, supporting its effectiveness in detecting ammonia release during the milk spoilage.

Hence, the sensor was used to monitor the ammonia emissions from five different brands of milk (labeled Milk 1 to Milk 5; detailed information about the milk samples is provided in Table S2), all measured at 40 % relative humidity. Fig. 13 (a) and 13(b) present the results for the ammonia vapor concentrations released from the five brands of milk after being exposed to air for 24 h (Day 1) and 120 h (Day 5), respectively. The data show an increase in light intensity for each milk sample, indicating a rise in ammonia content corresponding to the milk spoilage process. Furthermore, by analyzing the sensor response data with varying light wavelength values for all five milk samples, we carried out a classification of spoilage levels. The experimental results are presented in the PCA graph in Fig. 13(c), which illustrates the distinct classification of milk spoilage levels based on ammonia vapor emissions.

On Days 1 and 2, the data points in the PCA plot were still closely clustered, indicating the early stages of spoilage in the five milk samples. By Day 3, the data points were clearly separated, suggesting that all five milk cartons had fully spoiled. As the experiment continued, it became apparent that the spoilage process persisted, and the differences between the milk samples on Day 4 and Day 5 were clearly distinguishable. These observations were consistent with those of the pH value, which decreased from 6.93 on Day 1 to 5.58 on Day 5. When the light intensity values derived from ammonia vapor measurements of the five milk cartons over the five-day period (Fig. S5) were used to calculate the average sensing response value, as shown in Fig. 13(d), it was evident that the sensing response values increased daily. This indicates a progressive accumulation of ammonia due to the ongoing spoilage of the milk samples.

When the standard calibration equation was applied to calculate the ammonia concentrations on Day 1, the average values for Milk 3, Milk 4, and Milk 5 were found to be approximately 6.1 ppm, while Milk 1 and Milk 2 had lower concentrations of around 4.5 ppm. By Day 3, the ammonia concentrations in Milk 1 and Milk 2 had increased rapidly to about 7.0 ppm, approaching the levels observed in the other milk samples, which were around 8.0 ppm. This suggests that Milk 1 and Milk 2 experienced slower spoilage during the first two days compared to Milk 3, Milk 4, and Milk 5. However, by Day 3, all the milk samples had entered an advanced stage of microbial spoilage and continued to deteriorate. This was reflected in the steadily increasing ammonia concentrations, which reached 13.9 ppm by Day 5. The ammonia concentration values measured by the ZnO/R-CD sensors closely matched the 13.7 ppm measured by standard instruments, indicating the sensor's reliability in detecting spoilage.

ZnO thin films have been utilized for the detection of various amine vapors, including dimethylamine trimethylamine, diethylamine and triethylamine. These films exhibit significantly higher sensitivity and selectivity toward amines compared to ammonia [54,55]. This enhanced performance is attributed to the lower CN bond energies of the amines relative to the NH bond in ammonia, which facilitates the reduction of amine molecules on the ZnO surface more readily than that of ammonia vapor. These findings suggest a promising direction for future research, particularly in the development of optical sensing techniques for the detection of volatile amines as indicators of spoilage in meat and seafood products. Such approaches are expected to offer high sensitivity and low detection limits, making them well-suited for applications in food quality and safety monitoring at room temperature.

In this paper, we have presented a straightforward approach to the fabrication of ZnO/R-CD films. The spark method was used for ZnO and the hydrothermal method for R-CDs, which function as optical electronic noses utilizing the SPR technique for measurements at room temperature. The sensor response was found to be influenced by the thickness of the ZnO film layer. In our experiment, the ZnO film with optimal thickness was selected, and we demonstrated that the detection efficiency of the sensor could be enhanced under blue light stimulation. The sensor also exhibited cross-response capabilities, thus enabling the identification and differentiation of five VOCs (ammonia, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, and acetone) through the use of PCA analysis and varying the color of the light source on the smartphone screen. The sensor displayed the highest selectivity and response towards ammonia vapor. We also demonstrated the potential application of the sensor to the detection of milk spoilage by monitoring ammonia vapor emissions during the spoilage process. Given the advantages of the sensor developed here, such as its low cost, resistance to electromagnetic interference, and real-time monitoring capabilities, we believe that its potential applications extend to environmental monitoring, food quality analysis, and disease diagnosis.
